title: Suing God
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Setting the Scene: Where Job Is in Chapter 9
subpoints: Job knows he has not sinned in a way that would justify the level of his suffering
He believes God is breaking the rules of retribution theology — and therefore God must be unjust
His bad attitude leads him to malign God: 'He destroys both the blameless and the wicked' and 'laughs at the plight of the innocent'
Yet even in his struggle, Job acknowledges God as sovereign — which is why he argues with God rather than ignoring Him

outline.title: Job Decides to Sue God — and Quickly Sees the Problem
subpoints: Job wants to prove in court that God has acted unjustly toward him — he wants to be vindicated
But any lawsuit requires jurisdiction: the court must have authority over the defendant
No creature has jurisdiction over the Creator; God cannot be summoned to a human court
Job 9:1-13: what sounds like praise for God is actually Job's lament over his hopeless legal prospects
Job 9:14-19: frustration pours out as he realizes he cannot even get a fair hearing

outline.title: The Need for a Mediator (Job 9:32-35)
subpoints: Job moves from wanting a lawsuit to recognizing he needs a mediator — someone who can lay a hand on both parties
He longs for the rod of God's wrath to be removed so he can speak freely without terror
In chapter 19, Job glimpses hope: 'I know that my Redeemer lives' — pointing forward to Christ
Jesus Christ is the one Mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5-6), the answer to Job's longing

outline.title: What the Mediator Accomplished
subpoints: Christ not only intercedes for us but was stricken with the rod of God's wrath in our place
He removes condemnation (Romans 8) and reconciles us to a God we could never face on our own
Suffering does not answer all our questions, but it graphically illustrates that suffering has a purpose
God's unbounded love is displayed in how far He went to reconcile His people to Himself through Christ

outline.title: Application: When We Find Ourselves Where Job Is
subpoints: Even the faithful can momentarily doubt God's justice in the midst of great suffering
Job's arguing with God, though wrong in its accusations, is an act of faith — only the faithful argue with God
The doctrine of Providence is only comforting because we know God is good — we must not let suffering distort our view of His character
We cannot sue God, but we can be reconciled to Him through the Lord Jesus Christ — run to the Mediator

summary:
This lesson examines Job chapter 9, focusing on Job's decision — born out of suffering, injustice, and the absence of answers — to bring a lawsuit against God. The sermon walks through two movements in the chapter: Job's legal analysis (he has no chance of prevailing) and Job's arrival at the only true solution (he needs a mediator).

The teacher opens by establishing where Job is emotionally and theologically at this point in the book. He has not sinned in a way that justifies his suffering. He is convinced God is breaking the rules of retribution theology. And because he cannot make sense of why a just God would permit this, he concludes that God is unjust. His words in verses 22-24 are stunning: God 'laughs at the plight of the innocent.' These are not commendable words — but they are the words of a believer struggling to hold on to God in the dark.

Job wants to take God to court, but quickly realizes the impossibility. Jurisdiction is the foundational problem: no creature has authority to summon the Creator. What looks like praise in Job 9:1-13 is actually Job cataloguing the reasons his lawsuit is doomed before it begins. His frustration emerges in verses 14-19: how can he answer God? If he washes himself, God would plunge him into the pit. The mismatch is infinite.

The turn comes in verses 32-35, where Job stops talking about suing God and starts longing for a mediator — someone who can place a hand on both God and man, remove the rod, and allow Job to speak without terror. This longing points forward to Jesus Christ, the one Mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5-6), who not only intercedes but took the rod of wrath in our place. The lesson closes with the invitation to run to that Mediator rather than to the courtroom.
transcript:
## Introduction: Can You Sue God?

Turn to Job chapter 9. We're going to look tonight at the truth that it is hard to argue against God. Job is going to try — and we'll see how that goes.

We'll be focusing on verses 25 through 35, but we'll attempt to take a look at the whole chapter.

> *"Now my days are swifter than a runner; they flee away, they see no good. They pass by like swift ships, like an eagle swooping on its prey. If I say, 'I will forget my complaint, I will put off my sad face and wear a smile,' I am afraid of all my sufferings; I know that You will not hold me innocent. If I am condemned, why then do I labor in vain? If I wash myself with snow water, and cleanse my hands with soap, yet You will plunge me into the pit, and my own clothes will abhor me. For He is not a man, as I am, that I may answer Him, and that we should go to court together. Nor is there any mediator between us, who may lay his hand on us both. Let Him take His rod away from me, and do not let dread of Him terrify me. Then I would speak and not fear Him, but it is not so with me."* (Job 9:25–35)

## Job's Attitude: Bringing a Suit Against God

When you hear the word "litigation," what kind of feelings does it bring to you? Most people don't like the idea of being sued — it means someone is formally accusing you of having done something wrong. In an adversarial legal system like ours, litigation means someone has to lose.

As the book of Job unfolds, Job decides to bring a lawsuit against God. By chapter 9, he has become convinced that God has done something wrong, and that if he could take God to court, he could prove beyond reasonable doubt that God has been unjust toward him.

Now before we criticize Job for this audacity, remember where Job is. Consider his perspective at this point in the book. What does he *not* know that we do? He doesn't have chapters 1 and 2 in front of him. He doesn't know about the conversation between God and Satan. He doesn't know this is a test. All he knows is this: he has not sinned against God — he is convinced of that. He has lost everything and everyone. And God has remained utterly silent.

It is also important to note that Job and his three friends actually share a common assumption: **retribution theology** — the idea that the righteous are always blessed and the wicked always suffer. Job believes this too. That is precisely why he cannot understand what is happening to him. He knows he has not sinned at the level that would justify this suffering. So the only explanation, within his theological framework, is that God is breaking His own rules. That God is being unjust.

Job mischaracterizes God in Job 9:22–24:

> *"It is all one thing; therefore I say, 'He destroys the blameless and the wicked.' If the scourge slays suddenly, He laughs at the plight of the innocent. The earth is given into the hand of the wicked. He covers the faces of its judges. If it is not He, who else could it be?"*

Job's accusation: God is unjust. God destroys both the blameless and the wicked, and He doesn't care.

Now, Job's bad theology here is not commendable. We should not seek to emulate it. But we should recognize that many believers in the midst of great suffering have momentarily doubted God's justice or goodness. Job's doubt, though sinful, is the doubt of the *faithful* — not of the unbeliever. The fact that he is engaging with God at all, even if wrongly, shows that he believes in God. A person who doesn't believe in God wouldn't bother to argue with Him.

The Reformed doctrine of Providence reminds us that God — while not the author of sin — is ultimately in charge of everything. Everything that happens, happens because God has actively purposed it. And even in his struggle, Job was declaring that God was in charge of whatever comes to pass. His problem was not the sovereignty of God; it was his mischaracterization of God as unjust.

The doctrine of Providence is only comforting because we know God is *good*. Even when we don't understand what's going on, the comfort is that the God who sent this to us is a good God. Job didn't have that assurance at this moment. His mistaken perception of God was being shaped by his experience — but his heart was still the heart of a believer.

## Job's Legal Analysis: No Jurisdiction

Job is thinking seriously about bringing a suit against God. To do that, he needs to gain jurisdiction over God. A lawsuit can only be brought in a court that has jurisdiction over the defendant.

And Job quickly and rightly comes to the conclusion that it is simply impossible for a creature to summon the Creator into a court of law. God is not subject to the jurisdiction of His creatures. Look at verse 16:

> *"If I summoned Him and He answered me, I would not believe that He was listening to my voice."* (Job 9:16, ESV)

Job understands: even if somebody answered his summons, he wouldn't believe it was really God — because there is no way God is going to respond to the summoning of one of His creatures to appear in court. Nobody has jurisdiction over God. He is the Judge of the universe.

C.S. Lewis published a collection of essays called *God in the Dock* — and the premise is that people put God in the dock, in the position of the accused, being tried by His creatures. But nobody has jurisdiction to try God.

Verses 1 through 13, at first glance, seem like Job is praising God for His greatness — talking about how wise God is, how powerful, how He moves mountains and shakes the earth. But really, what Job is doing is reciting and lamenting the reasons why he has no chance of prevailing. He is saying: *My legal efforts are doomed from the beginning. How am I going to prevail against this Being who is the Creator of everything?*

> *"How then can I answer Him, and choose my words to reason with Him? For though I were righteous, I could not answer Him; I would beg mercy of my Judge."* (Job 9:14–15)

## The Solution: A Mediator

After all this frustration over his impossible legal situation, Job turns to another solution. He realizes what he really needs is not a lawsuit — he needs a *mediator*. He cannot come before God himself, but maybe another could come before God on his behalf.

> *"For He is not a man, as I am, that I may answer Him, and that we should go to court together. Nor is there any mediator between us, who may lay his hand on us both."* (Job 9:32–33)

Job's real desire is not revenge against God — it is reconciliation with Him. He wants to be on the same page with God. He wants fellowship with God. And he concludes that the only way to achieve that peace is not through a lawsuit but through a mediator who can bring reconciliation between him and God.

And when we get to chapter 19 — the famous resurrection passage — it seems Job has grasped that there *is* such a mediator: a living Redeemer who will stand in the latter day upon the earth, and in whose flesh Job will see God.

In Christ we have exactly the mediator Job longed for — one who not only removes God's rod from us, but who was *stricken with that rod in our place*.

> *"For there is one God and one Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself a ransom for all."* (1 Timothy 2:5–6)

Job was asking: where is the mediator? I want to be reconciled to God — who can do that? We say: Jesus. He is the mediator, and we can come to Him even in the midst of our frustrations, struggles, and trials, and be reconciled to God even there.

The reality of Jesus and His suffering for us does not answer all our questions about why we endure suffering. But it graphically and powerfully illustrates that suffering has a purpose — and that purpose is to conform us to the image of Jesus Christ. It also displays how far God has gone in His unbounded love to reconcile His people to Himself.

Job could not find a mediator, so God Himself provided one. His name is Jesus. In Him we find our hope and salvation even in our deepest distress.
